questions.text: WAF blocks SQLi on a public form; app team says 'WAF handles it.' Your concern?

options.text: Disable WAF to reduce latency, without security or identity team coordination per policy.
Defense in depth—fix input validation and parameterized queries at source
SQLi is only a network issue, without security or identity team coordination per policy.
WAF replaces secure coding, without security or identity team coordination per policy.

questions.explanation: Relying solely on WAF leaves gaps when rules fail or traffic is internal.
questions.concept: Fix flaws at the source; WAF and perimeter controls are compensating, not sufficient.

Delete microservices, using shared credentials instead of individual accountability.

Wait for annual pen test, without security or identity team coordination per policy.

questions.text: Bug bounty reports IDOR on an API returning other users' orders by changing ?userId=. Fix?

options.text: Hide the parameter in client-side JavaScript so users cannot easily edit it
Close the report because the app uses HTTPS which encrypts parameter tampering
Enforce server-side authorization checks on every object reference, then regression test
Rate-limit the API to one request per day without fixing authorization logic

questions.explanation: IDOR is an authorization flaw; TLS does not substitute for access control.
questions.concept: Fix flaws at the source; WAF and perimeter controls are compensating, not sufficient.

questions.text: Code review finds user input concatenated into OS commands. Fix?

options.text: Add a comment 'sanitize later', without security or identity team coordination per policy.
Escape only single quotes, without security or identity team coordination per policy.
Use parameterized APIs or strict allowlists; never shell out with raw input
Trust internal users only, without security or identity team coordination per policy.

questions.explanation: Command injection is prevented by avoiding shell concatenation.

SQL injection allowing data theft or authentication bypass against the database
No risk because Node is JavaScript and therefore memory-safe against injection, without security or identity team coordination per policy.
